speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two, and the survivor is Walter Austerer. We were talking about before the Nazis came into Vienna if there were any Nazis that you knew at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I told you about the doctor. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were a lot of others. But you know, I was 14 years old, just not even 14 when Hitler came in-- probably 13. And I had my friends and my own life and school life. So I really wasn't in contact with-- with the outside. And I didn't really-- well, unless I-- possibly I'm repressing it, but I don't think-- I mean, we talked about it constantly because we knew it was going on and we know what was happening in Germany and-- and what would be happening-- we had a pretty good idea what would be happening in Austria. So this wasn't something that was hidden. But personal experiences, I really didn't have where I, you know, met up with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anybody, or I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a-- a-- an inkling or a feeling of your parents or the people around you that there was a terrible thing coming? O--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. At-- you have to-- again, we need the time frame of when-- when are you-- what are you thinking about, what-- what period?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right before the Anschluss, before they came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the Anschluss?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh sure. Because there was a tremendous amount of tur-- turmoil. This was going on for a long time. The Nazis-- little by little, the illegal Nazis in Austria were-- were becoming legal, almost. They were coming to the surface. They were underground, then they were coming to the surface. And were-- there were riots and there were protest marches. And there were-- as a matter of fact, one of the first incidents that happened was my uncle-- and this is my only remaining aunt, who is her husband, who is now-- she is 95 years old. And her husband had a jewelery store, but also-- which inherited from his father. But also, his cousin-- or second cousin, I don't recall-- also had a store. And he-- maybe it was 1936, and somebody threw a bomb into that store, and he was killed. And-- so that was one of the first incidents, sort of a terrorist incident. And then other things happened. And there were constant provocations, and-- so yes, it was all around us at the time. But you lived with it and you still had hope that-- that Schuschnigg, who came in, who was a-- a Catholic government. I forgot now what they called the party, the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] social-- I forgot now. But it was Catholic-- basically a Catholic party, which-- which had-- which ran the government. So he was conservative, and-- and there was some hope that he would be able to maintain some kind of democracy. But when Hitler became stronger and stronger, it became very ominous. And then the threats, there with these constants threats of invasion and what he would do and so on. And then you saw Czechoslovakia. So yes, we were surrounded by it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you getting the news from what was going on in Germany? Was it readily--

speaker: interviewee
text: The newspapers. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned the legality-- and then-- the illegality and then the legality of the Nazi party. What were the circumstances that were around it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were outlawed. The Nazi party was outlawed. And then, when they put enormous pressure on Schuschnigg, the last chancellor, then he legalized them. And then, of course, they-- they were in a position of power, and that was the beginning of the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in maybe '37, the end of '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what-- did you notice-- what were the noticeable changes?

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, it was-- excuse me. It was a constant-- you know, I had never always talked about that. There was constant turmoil, and-- and-- and-- and I don't have the dates correct, but maybe it was 1933. Until then, it was a social, democratic government, a socialist-- basically a socialist government. There were communists, and there was some Nazis underground, but it was basically a socialist government. And then the fascists-- there was a-- a-- a coup-- there was a coup by Count [? Stahemberg ?] I think was his name. And I remember, when I was about 10 years old, and the socialists had built one of the-- the largest housing complexes in-- in Europe. It was the first public housing complex, and it was a beautiful housing complex for that time. And it was about oh, 15 minutes by train, by subway, from where I lived. And I could hear the machine guns they attacked-- when the fascists were attacking that housing pro-- project and during that coup. And I could hear, you know-- I could-- we could hear the machine guns going for a few days. And we-- I remember not being on the street, not being allowed to go out. And then, I don't think he himself ran the government. But then Dollfuss came in, and he was, I think, also part of the Catholic party. And then he was murdered a few years afterwards. He was murdered and shot. And so there was one upheaval after the other. And there was never-- af-- after that first coup in 1932, maybe, there was never a period of-- of peace or-- or quiet. So you-- you never-- you never felt relaxed. You never felt that this was a good place to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So going back to when the Nazi party was legalized, what were some of the changes that happened after then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there-- there weren't that many-- it all happened so quickly. It was-- it-- they were legalized very, very soon before-- just a few months before Hitler actually marched in. I-- I might not have my dates very exact, but it might have been the end of '37 and Hitler marched in in March '38. So there was-- as I said, there was constant turmoil and demonstrations and counter-demonstrations, and then Hitler marched in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about your memories of when Hitler did march in. What was it like? What were your personal observations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what it was like was that the first memory I have is of the-- the bombers coming in, flying in formation over-- over Vienna, and flying low. In those days, they flew low anyway. And the squadrons coming in, because the airport with just [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] again, 15 or 20 minutes away, by-- at the most, by car. And so we heard the-- the tanks and the troops. We could hear that all day and all night, or maybe longer than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do? Did you stay in your apartment? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a-- there was a presence of fear. Fear actually settled in at that, when I think back, you know. We-- I mean, we started to be afraid. And when before, we really hadn't been. We were anxious, but we were not afraid. At that point, you became afraid. And then he came in a few days later and made that speech at the Heldenplatz, the Hero's Place, and-- which is near the-- the inner castle, the-- the castle in-- in the inner city. And I don't know, maybe 200,000 people gathered. And that was about 15 minutes walking distance from where I lived, and you could hear the 'Heil Hitler' very clearly, that distance, welcoming him to-- into Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when that was happening, what kind of conversations did you have with your parents? What-- you know, what was the-- how did they talk to you about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- it was discussed and it was discussed of-- of beginning to talk about leaving the country. And you see, my father wanted to leave even in '36, or '35 or '36, because of the economic situation, which just before, an earlier problem, after he lost his job. And his brothers were here in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was ready to leave. He was ready to come to the United States. And he wrote to them, and they were telling him, you know, things are terrible here too. Do you want to come at the height of the Depression too? And they were struggling. They had families and they had businesses that they struggled with. So they said, you know, are you really thinking? Is that a good idea? And they didn't encourage him to come to the United States. So he never did. So at that point, there was-- after Hitler started, you know, we really started to talk about it and write to them again, and-- and-- then they sent-- then they sent the affidavits. Were there any restrictions put on you and your family and the other people, the other Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time? Well, the first, you know, thing that we saw, we saw the Nazis, the SS, and the SA came in. And-- and this was a very threatening presence. I mean, the-- the-- see the SS and the SA were a bunch of hoodlums. They were, you know, the lowest of the lowest to begin with. And that was the SA. And the SS was very ominous in their black uniforms and black boots. And-- so we were very scared at that point, of course. And then the first thing you knew they were making the women clean the swastikas off the streets that they had painted on with toothbrushes. You know, Jewish women, they had them clean off the swastikas and clean off the walls and all the slogans. That was their first action. And then they started the other stuff, beginning to take over Jewish stores. And eventually, I stopped school then, the-- the-- the middle of the year, because we know there was no sense of continuing anymore. And then-- then it was restricted and you couldn't go to school anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other activities that you remember the SS or the SA being involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just of taking over the stores and-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said, being a threatening presence. I remember one-- one incident which I-- I should mention. My father, as I told you, was in the restaurant supply business. And it became more and more difficult for him, and more dangerous for him, because he was not supposed to be doing it anymore. They-- they prohibited Jews from having stores. And even though he didn't have his store, he-- because he-- what he did, he would buy from the wholesaler and then sell directly himself. And my uncle was the manager of one of those store-- the big store, and that was the relationship with-- a business relationship, so.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he didn't really-- and the-- the office work, he did in our apartment. So he didn't have a store, but he wasn't allowed-- basically he was not allowed to do that anymore. So-- and one of the last acts was, there with a restaurant in-- in the suburbs of Vienna, in a hilly section, a wooded section. There was a trail leading up to the restaurant. There were many restaurants in these-- those hills.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had sold merchandise to them. And I don't recall whether he himself was fearful of going there. At that time, he was already very upset and he knew things were just falling in and their whole world was crumbling. And so I said to him, I'll take it. I'll deliver there. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he said OK, which I'm amazed at. And I put it in my rucksack, and I took the street car to wherever I had to get off. And then I had to walk through the woods on that path to that restaurant. And there I was, and I was walking. And all of a sudden an SS-- SA man came the other way, going downhill. I was going uphill. And for a moment, I was scared. Here I was in the middle of the words and this guy was coming down. So I thought very quickly. Didn't have-- but in those days, if anybody belonged-- if anybody my age belonged to the Hitler youth or whatever, they had a button that indi-- a swastika that indicated. And I didn't have that of course. But I did have my lederhosen. I had my blonde hair, my blue eyes. And so I raised my hand, I said, 'Heil Hitler,' and I passed. And I was scared-- scared as hell, because if he had stopped me, I mean, that would've been a very unpleasant, to say the least, experience. But I did go up and I delivered it and I came back. And-- but that was one of the last times that he actually sold anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell your father about that experience when you got home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time, what is your memory of the-- the reactions, or the actions, of the non-Jewish population of Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people who, like in our building or some of the Gentiles we knew, they-- they sort of [INAUDIBLE] they removed themselves. It was never dis-- basically not discussed. And it was sort of a-- a retreat. They sort of retreated into their own. Some of them, you know, were socialists before and really didn't believe in-- in what was going on, but just kept their mouth shut and didn't-- were afraid to support or give us any kind-- any kind of support. So it was more of a, you know, hidden agenda.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the activities where the Jewish population had to identify themselves? Were there any registrations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Sure. There were agencies. As a matter of fact, eventually we-- when things became very, very bad, and towards the end of-- as l said, you know, my father lost his business or couldn't continue his business anymore. And we wanted to save every penny we had because we knew we would be leaving the country one way or another.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then eventually, we registered for a soup kitchen, which was part of the Jewish community, which had organized it and where you got food. And that we did for a little while.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then what my father did-- he was a very, very handy man, and he also loved to build things. And he started-- he-- he built a trunk, a steamer trunk, in the apartment. And he built a streamer trunk for me. I still have it in the attic.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and it was very-- very beautiful. And people saw it. And everybody was beginning to emigrate. And they said, could you make one for us? And he started to build steamer trunks. And he did-- you know, made some money. And then we stopped, didn't have to go to the soup kitchen anymore, sort of supported us. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that helped. Did you have to carry any special papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that point, no. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you have to carry papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. I didn't. Not till-- that was after the war where you had to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any wearing of stars?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Not before the war. Not before the war. You made yourself as-- as invisible as possible. [LAUGHS] And, you know, when you saw an SS, you walked on the other side of the street. But that was about the only thing that happened. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any restrictions on your ability to travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Not that I know of. But we didn't travel anyway, so there was-- maybe there were, I don't know. Maybe there were. I-- I don't think there were, not till the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you were no longer going to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't go to school anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you occupy your time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I helped my father with the building of those trunks or whatever else, buying things, whatever else he had to do. And basically-- oh, I also-- yes, I also worked for my grandmother. My-- my grandfather had a tailor shop, and my grandmother continued it. And she had a couple of people working for them. And-- and it continued and supported her. And so I sort of apprenticed myself. Oh, then we moved. It must have been after we moved. We sold our apartment, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because we knew that we were-- we would have to leave. And so we sold our apartment and moved in with my grandmother, who had extra rooms. And then I sort of apprenticed myself out to that tailor. And I learned to do all kinds of things, and was very helpful. I can still sew. So that was one thing. And then I went to the-- it lasted a little bit. And then I went to the Jewish organization that I mentioned before, the Zionist organization, which were-- which was preparing kids to-- for going to Palestine. And I went there, and that was sort of a club-- club affair, you know. It was more than just the-- it prepared us in many ways. I remembered we got lec-- we had lectures about health. And my first sex lecture was given there. So they introduced us to a lot of different areas, prepared us to, you know, to go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and who-- who ran the organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Zionist group. I don't remember which-- which arm it was. I have no idea whether it was Jabotinsky or another one. I don't remember that anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it your intention at that time that you and your family would go to-- to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was one possibility, yeah. Yeah. Except that, you know, we knew it was all illegal. They were the illegal transport. There was no legal way of getting into Palestine. So it was based on the hope that somehow you would get on one of those illegal ships.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned before your extended family and all the cousins that you had. What was happening to them at this point? Were they staying, or were they emigrating to some place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were doing both. Whoever could emigrate would emigrated. My-- the first one to leave was an uncle, Uncle Willy. And I, as a matter of fact, liked him very much because he was a very outgoing guy, very free spirit. And I went with him to the airport, which was a dirt strip in those days. And I remember him taking off in one of those old planes into Switzerland. Eventually he wound up in London. He was the first one. And then my second one would have been my-- another uncle. They went to England as a married-- what they called married couple going into household. They would let-- if you became household help, they would let some people come in. And their children also went too, on Kindertransport. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Others had more difficulty. And then eventually many were killed in-- in the camps. One of my-- matter of fact, when I was in Prague just a couple weeks ago, I met a cousin of mine whom I hadn't seen since 1939 in London. And her parents were sent to the-- they had no hope of going anywhere, and they-- they were sent to the ghetto in Lodz, and they were killed there. My grandmother and her unmarried sister-- her ummarried daughter, who-- Helene, who had been living with her, they were sent to Theresienstadt. My grandmother was-- died on the way, on the train to Theresienstadt. Her daughter was killed in Theresienstadt. Another uncle and aunt also went. I'm not sure what ghetto they went to. Might have been Lodz too. They were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the grandmother who you were living with.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when she was taken to Theresienstadt? I don't know the exact date, but it was maybe 1941 or so. So you were already-- you were already out. And how did you eventually find out about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle found out, the one uncle who was sort of the pater familias. And he found out after the war that that's what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm trying to think of the other family. So about half the family was-- was killed in the camps. My father's brother in Romania, in Czernowitz, his family was-- he and his family was taken to Siberia to work in a mine, and they died in Siberia. So-- and-- and of course the children were killed too. The-- the time now, you're living in your grandmother's house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Do you-- could you bring to mind, what-- what are the memories that you have of the-- of the-- of the events that led up to Kristallnacht? What-- what was-- what was happening at that time prior to Kristallnacht, and-- and when Kristallnacht itself started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the-- we had already moved to my grandmother's house-- apartment. So we didn't live across the temple anymore, which would have been pretty horrible if we had. So we were a little bit removed from it. But we heard it on the-- maybe on the radio, on the-- communications were not the way they are today. But we heard it on the radio, maybe in the newspaper, or maybe just by word of mouth what was going on, that vom Rath had killed the German attache, I think, in Paris. That's what's precipitated it. And-- I mean, the-- the Jewish man had killed vom Rath in Paris. And so we knew that had happened. We knew that had happened. And then they were beginning, I think, even immediately-- I don't know what the time frame was between the killing and Kristallnacht. I don't know how many-- whether one or two days passed or whatever. But that there was already talk and, you know, with the grape vine. And it happened very quickly, because the town is not that large, and-- and rumors spread very quickly, that they were rounding up men for a first-- that was the first time-- Jewish men and putting them into concentration camps. And so the-- the morning, and then maybe they burned-- and I don't remember whether they burned the synagogues at night or whether they did it on-- I think they burned them the day before, or the night before. And evidently, my father must have been instrumental, and I don't remember that. But as I said before, the-- the janitor in our building, in my grandmother's building, was a Social Democrat. And [INAUDIBLE] my father must have, I would assume, gone to her and said, we have to hide. And so then she suggested that he-- he and I should go into the coal cellar. And so that's what we did. And-- excuse me-- and if we-- if she had been a Nazi, she would have said no. You know, you can't-- they wouldn't have given us any-- told us about any place. I didn't even know whether we knew that the coal cellar existed or not. And so that's what we did. We went into the coal cellar, and we were hiding all day and just standing there. There were no chairs or anything. I remember just standing and looking out this little bitty window that they had. And then, I assume, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother or my-- my mother must have come down at the end of the day and told us all right, that we could come up. The SS had come to the apartment and my grandmother opened the door, and they'd asked for the men. And she said there were no men here, and-- and they left. I don't even remember whether they went through the apartment or not. They might have. And they were going through the old Jewish apartments and rounding up the men. And then they were taken to Dachau. And a friend of my father's, I remember, a close friend of his was taken to the camp. And so we escaped that time. We went up the next day, and then things simmered down again.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop here for a moment.
